Direct mail, mailing your customers still works. In fact, it's working better than it ever has before. And the reason is, and the reason is because fewer and fewer business owners are doing it. So your mail piece stands out. So if you really want to amp up this system, you will do both email and direct mail. Because the truth is a lot of people, no matter how good your email is, guess what? They're not reading it. I know folks who don't check their email for over a week. To me, I find that amazing. I check my email at least once a day, twice a day, three times a day, four times a day, maybe even more. But there are some people that don't. So what you need to do, if you really want to amp up this system, is to do both direct mail and email. Okay, let's go over the day one email and the psychology behind the day one email. The first thing that you're going to do is you're going to tell a story that proves a concept. Tell a story. Storytelling, by the way, is one of the most powerful forms of marketing. And very few business owners really know how to do it. Here's something for you to remember. Stories invite interest while statements invite skepticism. Let me repeat that. Stories invite interest while statements invite skepticism. So you want to tell a story. And you'll notice in the samples that we've given you, we are telling a story. So what does the story talk about? Well, it talks about a problem that your customer, that your prospect, your customer client or patient, actually they're not a prospect or a customer, client or patient, is something that they can relate to and it introduces a solution. So it sets up the problem that your customer can relate to and then it introduces a solution. You've got the problem and then the solution. And at the end of this email, you have a PS or a note that leads to your solution, to the solution of the problem. But the real goal of the day one email is to get them to read that first email. Alright, so let me just review. You're going to tell a story. The second thing is the story talks about a problem that your customer can relate to and it introduces a solution. At the end, you have a PS or a note that leads to the solution to the problem. Them either buying something, calling in for an appointment, downloading something, whatever it is. But the real goal of day one, it's not really selling them anything, is to get them to read the email. Now let's go into day two in the psychology behind day two. This is an email that introduces your solution to the problem. Now in the first email, you talk more about what the problem is. In this email, you're really talking about the solution to the problem. Some of the key points are it references the previous day's email. And when you go through the sample, you'll see that this is exactly what it does. And we didn't want you to miss it, so we want to give you this step-by-step instruction. The second thing is you need to sell the outcome or the transformation. Sell the outcome or the transformation. Well, what does that mean? You need to let your audience know. You need to let your customer know that the outcome or the transformation that this is going to provide really works and it's really something that they want. Now a powerful way to do that is to mention a guarantee. To have a guarantee. You can also have a bonus. Let's talk a little bit about guarantees just for a second. Because every business can offer some type of guarantee. Now you may not be able to guarantee the outcome of your service, for example, but you can guarantee a part of your service. So for example, my doctor has a guarantee that if I have an appointment, I will be in within five minutes of my appointment time. That's his guarantee. Now he can't guarantee me that if I'm sick, he's going to make me better, but he can guarantee that. So think about, is there something in your business that you can guarantee? Now maybe there's a bonus that you want to offer. Bonuses are very powerful. Bonuses help move the sale. So is there something cool that you can offer? By the way, it can be an unrelated bonus. I once worked with an estate planning attorney who during Valentine's Day had this entire promotion that if a husband and wife came to her office for an initial consultation about an estate plan, they would get a dinner for two at a romantic Italian restaurant. That was a bonus offer. The goal of the day two email is to get your customer to go to the quote sales page. Now the reason I say the quote sales page is maybe you are selling an actual product. That's fantastic. That's what we often do at GKIC. But it doesn't necessarily mean that you're selling a product. You could be offering a consultation and the sales page offers them the consultation and gives them instructions about how to take advantage of the consultation that you're offering. You could be offering them a special romantic evening at your restaurant as another example. So the sales page doesn't necessarily need to be selling a product. It needs to be selling the next step in your process. So let's again review the day two email. This is an email that introduces your solution to the problem that you talked about the day before. It does reference the previous day's email. It sells the outcome or the transformation that you're saying that the person is going to have. It mentions a bonus and a guarantee. So think about what can I offer as a bonus? What can I offer as a guarantee? And the goal of the day two email is to get them to click on a link and go to a sales page where the continuation happens, whatever happens next in your process. Now let's move on to day number three. Day three email. Again, as an email number two, it references the previous email. And this email highlights the bonuses and sells the bonus. So remember, we offer it a bonus in the second email. And this email, what we're doing is we're highlighting that bonus and maybe even adding on an additional bonus. Sometimes people will take action just to take advantage of the bonus that you offer. So by the way, your bonus could be a special report. It can be a book. It could be, as I just mentioned, a dinner for two. You have to open up your mind to the possibilities of what bonuses could I offer? What are some things that my customers, clients, or patients would really, really want? The next thing is, and this is really important, you want to discuss what will happen if they don't buy. As Tony Robbins teaches, people are motivated by twin opposing forces, pain and pleasure. Right now, in the first two emails, we've been basically talking about all the pleasure that they'll get by taking advantage of the opportunity that you're presenting to them. In this one, we're going to use pain. We're going to talk about what will happen if they don't take advantage of the opportunity that you're giving them. And again, you can see how this is all laid out in the samples that we've given you. The next thing is you want to overcome objections. One of the most important things that you can do in any type of selling, and by the way, make no doubt about it, you are actually selling here. Now, you're not selling face to face. You're using email as your selling tool, but you're selling. I don't know a way to create an instant cash flow search without making an offer and without selling. Really important. So, one of the most important things that you can do in any type of sales presentation is make a list of all of the possible objections that your customer might have. Why wouldn't they take advantage of this opportunity? And then you want to work out in advance how you could answer those objections. So, in this email, the day three email, you answer the objections. Why wouldn't somebody take advantage of your opportunity? And then you have the all important PS. Now, the PS in the day three email highlights the deadlines. We didn't talk a lot about deadlines. We will in a little bit, but deadlines are really mission critical. If there's no deadline, there's no sense of urgency. Remember, we're talking about here people who weren't expecting to give you any money. We're asking them to take action and give you additional money that they weren't expecting, that they weren't planning on. If you don't have a deadline, if there's no sense of urgency, either in time or in quantity, then it's going to be very difficult to move people to action. And this is all about moving massive amounts of people to action as quickly as possible. So, the time deadline is based around, well, a date. This thing goes away on Friday. We do a lot of that at GKIC. So, we'll do something called the Web Deal of the Weekend. And, well, guess what? It runs over the weekend and it expires on Monday. Everyone knows because in the email, we say it expires on Monday. And then once it's gone, it's gone. What we'll notice is a surge of additional sales right at the deadline. People have been thinking about it. They've got the deadline in their mind. The other thing is a quantity deadline. So, let's say that you're selling a book or you're selling a consultation. Let's just use consultation as an example. You're selling a consultation, right? Well, there's literally only so many that you can do. So, you can say this is for the first 10 people. And once that's done, it's done. So, that's what you do in the day three email. You reference a previous email. You highlight the bonuses. You discuss what will happen if they don't buy. You overcome objections and then the PS highlights the deadline. Okay, now let's talk about a really important subject. And we've kind of skirted around it, but it's about using direct response marketing and all of the marketing that you're doing, not just in the cash flow surge sequence that we've given you here. This is one of the most important sections of this training. So, what makes direct response direct response? And what's the difference between direct response and image advertising? Well, most business owners are doing image advertising. They look at the big advertisers and the people that are spending millions or even billions of dollars on marketing and they say, well, those companies are really successful. Therefore, I should model what they're doing. You can't do that. You see as an entrepreneur, as a small business owner, when we put money out, we need that money to come back fairly quickly and bring friends with it, right? A image advertiser doesn't care about that because they can spend a whole bunch of money on just getting their name out there. You can't do that. You shouldn't do that. It's a big waste of money. So, let's talk about what makes direct response direct response. The first thing is in every direct response marketing piece, whether it's a newspaper ad, whether it's a letter on a website, whether it's the first thing that comes out of your mouth, it's a headline. The headline is what we call the ad for your ad. Now, in an email, the headline is, well, the subject line. You see, there's really nothing new under the sun. The subject line or the headline is mission critical because that's what gets people interested in reading more. So, if it's in a newspaper ad or on a postcard or something like that, the headline gets them to read the rest. The subject line gets them to click and open the email. So, you need to spend time on creating powerful subject lines and powerful headlines. And you'll see exactly how we did that in the samples that are included with this kit. The next thing we talked about is telling a story. Telling a story. We've already gone over this in detail, and I encourage you to listen to this more than once so you can get all the nuances. Storytelling is one of the most powerful marketing tools that you can use because it engages your prospect. It engages your customer. Because as children, we've been taught, hey, let me tell you a story. Let me tell you a story. What happens? We perk up. We listen. It's ingrained in us. The next thing, and this is where most people mess up, is in the offer or the value proposition. What I'm about to say could really change so much for you. Now, there's a whole bunch more we need to talk about here as it comes to offer. But for this training, if you just get this one thing, you'll be light years ahead of your competition. Here's what it is. You're not selling your product. You're not selling your service. You're selling the outcome. You're selling the transformation. Let me repeat that. Regardless of what you're selling, you're not selling your product or service. You're selling the outcome that your customer wants, that your customer needs, or that your customer desires. It is such an important concept. So, for example, when I saw the founder, Dan Kennedy, speak, he wasn't selling me the toolkit with the copyright free marketing documents and the CDs and all of that stuff. He wasn't selling me that. What he was selling me on was the fact that if I took this toolkit and I used it, I would explode my business. I would make more money. I would book more shows. I would be successful. That's what he was selling me. He wasn't selling me on the actual product. If you can get this and change your offers to focus on the outcome that your prospect wants, it's going to make your marketing so much more powerful. The next thing, as far as direct response goes, is you need what we call a CTA. A CTA. Write that down, circle it, and red put stars next to it. CTA stands for a call to action. A call to action. We are looking for, well, a direct response. We're looking for people to take an action. We're not looking to just get our name out there and hopefully let them remember us. We're telling them specifically what we want them to do. And your call to action must be precise and detailed. Tell them exactly what you want them to do. If you want them to pick up the phone, tell them to pick up the phone and put out their index finger and dial these numbers. If you want them to click on a link, tell them to click on the link. If you want them to come into your store on Sunday between 10 and 11 to get the special bonus, tell them, hey, put it on your calendar right now. Set your alarm. So you're in the store, so you get the special bonus on Saturday between 10 and 11. Tell them specifically what you want them to do. The next thing that makes direct response, direct response, in most cases, there's some bonuses. We talked a lot about bonuses, but bonuses can help drive the sale. Also, in direct response, something we've talked about are guarantees. What can you guarantee in your business? If you immediately say nothing, take a step back, take a pause, take a breath, and think about what part of my product, what part of my service can I actually guarantee? And then the other thing that makes direct response, direct response are deadlines. We talked a lot about deadlines. That happens in email number three. Now, by the way, you probably mentioned your deadlines in the other emails as well, but in email number three, you really focus on the deadline when this thing is going away. So again, direct response, you have a headline. There's a story. There's an offer. Remember what the offer is about. Really important. There's a CTA, a call to action. There may be bonuses. There may be a guarantee, but there's a deadline. Now, what type of offer does this cash flow surge system work for? Well, it works for if you're running some type of special.
